summary: Implement a nightly Azure Function (Python 3.11, Linux Consumption plan) that queries GPT-4o via TRAPI to retrieve the 1985 New York Yankees roster and writes the result to an Azure Storage Blob container. All authentication uses managed identity — no API keys. Infrastructure is provisioned via Bicep. CI/CD runs through GitHub Actions.
assumptions: Python 3.11 is the target runtime based on existing infra conventions in the repository.
Azure Functions Linux Consumption plan is the target hosting model.
TRAPI endpoint and GPT-4o model deployment details are available to the implementation team (not stored as plain-text secrets).
Blob container name is 'yankees-roster' per existing Bicep conventions.
Managed Identity (system-assigned) on the Function App is used for both AzureWebJobsStorage and TRAPI auth; no API keys are stored anywhere.
AzureWebJobsStorage is configured via AzureWebJobsStorage__accountName (identity-based, no connection string).
RBAC assignments: Storage Blob Data Owner and Queue Data Contributor on the storage account.
Application Insights is provisioned for observability.
GitHub Actions is the CI/CD platform.
Bicep is the IaC framework; infra lives under /infra.
The nightly schedule is 00:00 UTC (cron: '0 0 * * *') unless overridden.
Test framework is pytest with pytest-cov; minimum coverage threshold is 80%.
The task plan does not require explicit approval — it is automatically approved per planning conventions.
open_questions: What is the exact TRAPI endpoint URL and GPT-4o deployment name that should be used?
Should the blob output be a single overwritten file (e.g., roster.json) or a timestamped file per run?
Is there a required JSON schema / format for the stored roster data?
What Azure subscription and resource group should the resources be deployed to?
Are there any network restrictions (Private Endpoints, VNet integration) required?
What is the desired nightly execution time (timezone / UTC offset)?
What alert notification channel should be used (email, Teams webhook, PagerDuty)?
What is the minimum acceptable test coverage percentage?
Is there an existing Azure AD application registration for TRAPI, or does one need to be created?

waves.name: Foundation
waves.objective: Provision all cloud infrastructure and establish CI/CD pipeline so the team has a stable, deployable target environment before writing application code.
entry_criteria: Azure subscription and resource group confirmed
TRAPI endpoint and GPT-4o deployment name confirmed
GitHub repository access and secrets configured
exit_criteria: Bicep deployment succeeds with no errors
Function App, Storage Account, and Application Insights are provisioned
Managed Identity is active and RBAC assignments are in place
CI/CD pipeline runs end-to-end (lint + test + deploy) on main branch
No plaintext secrets exist in code or pipeline configuration

waves.name: Core Build
waves.objective: Implement the TRAPI/GPT-4o data fetch, blob writer, and Azure Function timer trigger so that the nightly job produces a stored roster.
entry_criteria: Local dev environment can authenticate to Azure via managed identity or az login
TRAPI client module exists or is scaffolded
exit_criteria: Timer-triggered Azure Function executes nightly (cron 0 0 * * *)
GPT-4o returns 1985 Yankees roster via TRAPI
Roster is written to the 'yankees-roster' blob container
Unit tests pass with >= 80% coverage
Integration test confirms end-to-end blob write

waves.name: Hardening
waves.objective: Add reliability controls, structured observability, and security hardening so the nightly job is production-grade.
entry_criteria: Core Build wave exit criteria met
exit_criteria: Retry logic with exponential backoff implemented for TRAPI calls
Structured JSON logs emitted and visible in Application Insights
Alert fires within 5 minutes of a failed function execution
No API keys or secrets in code, environment variables use identity-based auth
Security scan (bandit / safety) passes with no high-severity findings

waves.name: Release Readiness
waves.objective: Validate end-to-end in a staging slot or pre-production run, complete documentation, and confirm operational readiness before declaring the project complete.
entry_criteria: All unit and integration tests passing in CI
exit_criteria: Nightly function executes successfully in production at least once
Blob container contains a valid roster file
README documents deployment steps, configuration, and runbook
All success criteria in spec.md checked off
Post-deploy monitoring dashboard reviewed by owner

tasks.title: Review and approve implementation task plan
tasks.description: Distribute this task plan to stakeholders for review. Confirm assumptions, resolve open questions (TRAPI endpoint, blob schema, schedule timezone, alert channel), and obtain sign-off before implementation begins.
outputs: Resolved open questions documented in team wiki or issue
acceptance_criteria: All open questions listed in the plan have documented answers
Task plan is approved by rciapala or designated delegate
Any revised assumptions are captured and communicated to the team
risks: TRAPI endpoint details unavailable — blocks INF-02 and APP-01
Unresolved blob schema question may require rework of APP-02

tasks.title: Provision Azure Storage Account and blob container via Bicep
tasks.description: Author or update Bicep module under /infra/modules/storage.bicep to provision a Storage Account (LRS, Standard) and the 'yankees-roster' blob container. Tag resources with project=1985-NY-Yankees and owner=rciapala.
inputs: Azure subscription and resource group details
outputs: Storage Account resource ID output from Bicep
acceptance_criteria: Storage Account is provisioned with LRS redundancy
'yankees-roster' blob container exists with private access
Resources are tagged project=1985-NY-Yankees, owner=rciapala
Bicep deployment completes with no errors or warnings
Storage Account name is surfaced as a Bicep output
risks: Storage Account name collisions if naming convention not enforced
Region availability constraints

tasks.title: Provision Azure Function App with system-assigned managed identity
tasks.description: Author or update /infra/modules/functionapp.bicep to deploy a Linux Consumption Function App (Python 3.11). Enable system-assigned managed identity. Configure AzureWebJobsStorage__accountName (identity-based, no connection string). Wire Application Insights instrumentation key.
outputs: Function App name as Bicep output
acceptance_criteria: Function App is deployed on Linux Consumption plan with Python 3.11 runtime
System-assigned managed identity is enabled and principal ID is non-null
AzureWebJobsStorage__accountName is set; no AzureWebJobsStorage connection string exists
APPLICATIONINSIGHTS_CONNECTION_STRING app setting is configured
Function App health probe returns 200
risks: Cold-start latency on Consumption plan may cause first-run delays
Identity-based AzureWebJobsStorage requires correct RBAC to be in place before first invocation

tasks.title: Provision Application Insights workspace
tasks.description: Add or update a Bicep module to provision an Application Insights resource (workspace-based) linked to a Log Analytics workspace. Output the connection string for use by the Function App.
acceptance_criteria: Application Insights resource is provisioned and linked to a Log Analytics workspace
Connection string is available as a Bicep output
Live Metrics stream is accessible in Azure Portal
risks: Log Analytics workspace quota limits in free tier subscriptions

tasks.title: Assign RBAC roles for managed identity on storage and TRAPI
tasks.description: Author or update /infra/modules/rbac.bicep to assign Storage Blob Data Owner and Storage Queue Data Contributor roles to the Function App managed identity on the Storage Account. Confirm that TRAPI access also uses identity-based auth (no API key).
outputs: No plaintext credentials in any config
acceptance_criteria: Storage Blob Data Owner role assigned to Function App managed identity on the storage account
Storage Queue Data Contributor role assigned to Function App managed identity on the storage account
No storage connection strings or API keys present in Function App configuration
TRAPI authentication is confirmed to use managed identity or Azure AD token (no API key)
RBAC assignments verified via Azure Portal or az role assignment list
risks: TRAPI may not support managed identity auth — escalate to open question resolution
Over-permissive roles if Blob Data Owner scope is not tightly bounded

tasks.title: Set up GitHub Actions CI/CD pipeline
tasks.description: Create a GitHub Actions workflow (.github/workflows/deploy.yml) that runs lint, pytest with coverage, Bicep validation, and deploys the Function App on push to main. Use federated identity (OIDC) or GitHub secret for Azure login — no stored service principal passwords.
inputs: Azure service principal or OIDC federation setup
outputs: Automated deployment to Function App on merge to main
acceptance_criteria: Pipeline triggers on push to main and on pull requests
Lint (flake8 or ruff) and pytest steps run and must pass before deploy
pytest --cov report is uploaded as a workflow artifact
Bicep templates are validated (az deployment validate) in the pipeline
Deployment to Function App succeeds on merge to main
No service principal passwords stored as plain GitHub secrets (OIDC preferred)
risks: OIDC federation setup requires Azure AD configuration that may need elevated permissions
Cold deploy may time out on first Consumption plan deployment

tasks.title: Implement TRAPI client to fetch 1985 Yankees roster from GPT-4o
tasks.description: Implement or extend trapi_client.py to call the TRAPI endpoint with GPT-4o, sending a prompt that retrieves the complete 1985 New York Yankees roster. Use Azure AD token-based authentication (no API key). Return a structured list of player objects.
inputs: TRAPI endpoint URL and GPT-4o deployment name (from PROJ-01)
outputs: Unit tests for TRAPI client with mocked HTTP responses
acceptance_criteria: fetch_1985_yankees_roster() returns a list of player dicts with at least name and position fields
Authentication uses DefaultAzureCredential or ManagedIdentityCredential — no API key
Unit tests cover success path, empty response, and HTTP error scenarios
Tests pass: pytest tests/ -v --cov=trapi_client with >= 80% coverage on the module
Function raises a descriptive exception on non-2xx TRAPI response
risks: GPT-4o response format may be inconsistent — prompt engineering may be needed
TRAPI rate limits or quota may throttle nightly executions
Managed identity token acquisition adds latency on cold start

tasks.title: Implement blob writer to store roster to Azure Storage
tasks.description: Implement blob_writer.py with a write_roster_to_blob() function that serializes the roster list to JSON and uploads it to the 'yankees-roster' container using DefaultAzureCredential (no connection string). Blob name should be configurable (e.g., roster.json or timestamped).
acceptance_criteria: write_roster_to_blob() uploads valid JSON to 'yankees-roster' container
Authentication uses DefaultAzureCredential — no connection string or account key
Blob name strategy (overwrite vs. timestamped) is implemented per resolved open question
Unit tests cover success, overwrite, and BlobServiceClient error scenarios
Tests pass: pytest tests/ -v --cov=blob_writer with >= 80% coverage
risks: Blob overwrite without versioning loses historical data — timestamped naming mitigates this
Incorrect RBAC scope causes 403 errors at runtime

tasks.title: Implement Azure Function timer trigger (nightly cron)
tasks.description: Implement the Azure Function entry point (function_app.py or equivalent) with a timer trigger configured to run nightly (cron: '0 0 * * *'). The function calls fetch_1985_yankees_roster() then write_roster_to_blob(), logs structured output, and handles exceptions gracefully.
acceptance_criteria: Timer trigger is configured with schedule '0 0 * * *' (midnight UTC)
Function successfully invokes fetch_1985_yankees_roster() and write_roster_to_blob()
Structured log entries (JSON) are emitted at INFO level for success and ERROR level for failures
Function completes within 5 minutes (well within the 10-minute Consumption plan timeout)
Manual invocation via Azure Portal or func host start succeeds locally
Integration test validates end-to-end flow with real or stubbed TRAPI and blob
risks: Consumption plan cold start may delay first nightly execution
Timer trigger does not retry automatically on failure — retry logic must be in app code

tasks.title: Implement retry logic and failure handling for TRAPI calls
tasks.description: Add retry logic with exponential backoff and jitter to the TRAPI client for transient HTTP errors (429, 500, 503). Define a max retry count (e.g., 3) and a circuit-breaker pattern or dead-letter fallback to prevent endless spinning.
inputs: Azure Functions host.json retry policy docs
outputs: Updated trapi_client.py with retry decorator or tenacity-based retry
acceptance_criteria: Retry logic retries on HTTP 429, 500, 503 with exponential backoff (base 2s, max 30s)
Max 3 retries before raising a terminal exception
Retry attempts are logged at WARNING level with attempt number
Unit tests verify retry count and backoff behavior using mocked responses
No infinite retry loops possible
risks: Aggressive retries may exhaust TRAPI quota
Total retry duration must stay within Function App execution timeout

tasks.title: Configure structured logging and Application Insights telemetry
tasks.description: Configure the Azure Function to emit structured JSON logs via the Azure Functions logging framework. Add custom dimensions (run_id, player_count, duration_ms) to Application Insights traces. Ensure all exceptions are captured as Application Insights exceptions.
inputs: Azure Monitor OpenTelemetry or applicationinsights SDK
outputs: Structured logs visible in Application Insights Logs (traces table)
acceptance_criteria: Each function invocation emits a structured log entry with run_id, player_count, and duration_ms
Exceptions are captured in Application Insights exceptions table
Logs are queryable in Log Analytics via KQL within 5 minutes of execution
No PII or secrets appear in log output
risks: Log volume on Consumption plan may incur unexpected costs if verbosity is too high

tasks.title: Configure alerting for failed function executions
tasks.description: Create an Azure Monitor alert rule that fires when the function execution count with result 'Failed' is > 0 in a 15-minute window. Route the alert to the confirmed notification channel (email / Teams / PagerDuty).
outputs: Azure Monitor alert rule deployed (via Bicep or ARM)
acceptance_criteria: Alert rule fires within 5 minutes of a failed function execution
Alert is routed to the designated notification channel
Alert rule is deployed as IaC (Bicep) — not manually created
Test alert triggered manually and notification received
risks: Alert notification channel not confirmed — blocks this task
Alert suppression during first-run testing may mask real failures

tasks.title: Run security scan and remediate high-severity findings
tasks.description: Run bandit (SAST) and pip-audit / safety (dependency vulnerability scan) against the codebase. Remediate any high-severity findings. Add scans as a required CI/CD pipeline step.
outputs: Security scan step added to GitHub Actions workflow
acceptance_criteria: bandit -r . reports zero high-severity issues
pip-audit reports no known critical CVEs in dependencies
Security scan steps are mandatory (blocking) in the CI pipeline
No hardcoded credentials, tokens, or connection strings detected
risks: Third-party dependency vulnerabilities may require version pinning that conflicts with Azure SDK requirements

tasks.title: Write and validate integration tests for end-to-end pipeline
tasks.description: Write integration tests that invoke the full pipeline (TRAPI fetch → blob write) against a real or emulated Azure environment (Azurite for local blob emulation). Validate that the blob container receives a valid JSON roster file.
acceptance_criteria: Integration test invokes the function end-to-end and asserts blob exists in 'yankees-roster' container
Integration test validates blob content is valid JSON with at least one player entry
Tests run in CI against Azurite or a dedicated test storage account
Overall test coverage >= 80% reported by pytest-cov
risks: TRAPI not available in CI — mock or stub must be used for automated runs
Azurite version compatibility with azure-storage-blob SDK version

tasks.title: Write README with deployment steps, configuration, and runbook
tasks.description: Author a README.md covering: project overview, local development setup, environment variables / app settings, deployment steps (az deployment + GitHub Actions), manual trigger instructions, and an operational runbook (how to check logs, re-run manually, rotate nothing — no keys).
acceptance_criteria: README covers local dev setup with az login and func host start instructions
README lists all required app settings and their purpose (no values hardcoded)
Deployment section covers both infra (bicep) and app (GitHub Actions) steps
Runbook section covers how to check Application Insights logs and manually trigger the function
README reviewed and approved by rciapala
risks: Documentation goes stale if written before final app settings are confirmed

tasks.title: Validate production deployment and first nightly run
tasks.description: Deploy to production, manually trigger the function once, and verify the 'yankees-roster' blob container contains a valid roster file. Confirm Application Insights shows a successful execution trace. Check off all spec.md success criteria.
outputs: Signed-off success criteria checklist in spec.md
Screenshot or KQL query result showing successful run
acceptance_criteria: Function executes successfully in production (manual trigger)
'yankees-roster' blob container contains a valid JSON roster file with 1985 Yankees players
Application Insights shows successful execution trace with player_count > 0
Nightly timer trigger fires on schedule (verified after first overnight run)
All spec.md success criteria checkboxes are marked complete
No alerts fired during the validation run
risks: TRAPI availability in production environment not yet verified
Timer trigger first fire may be delayed by up to 1 minute on Consumption plan
